description: A course of events or recurring practice is 'sanowu' when information available beforehand lets someone reasonably anticipate how or when it will unfold. A meeting may have predictable stages while its exact length remains uncertain. 'koluri' (reliable) concerns repeated performance; 'sanowu' concerns what can be known ahead of time.
articulatory_notes: Raise the tongue blade towards the alveolar ridge for unvoiced 's', direct air through the narrow channel, and open into central 'a'. Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for voiced dental 'n', carry the voice through the nose, and round the lips into stressed true-mid back 'o'. Keep the lips rounded and the back of the tongue raised for voiced 'w', then finish with high rounded back 'u'.
sound_symbolism: The first two syllables of 'sanowu' repeat the complete sound of 'sano' (know). Final 'wu' keeps the lips rounded and glides into deep 'u', an audible kinship that suits something knowable before it arrives.
usage_notes: The observer and information source can appear in the surrounding clause, along with the time horizon and remaining uncertainty. Noun use supplies predictability. The word supports preparation but never turns an expectation into certainty.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Predictable timing gives a participant room to prepare instead of revealing every demand when an answer is already expected.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Seasonal work reads recurring signs while leaving weather, birth, harvest, and illness free of the clockmaker's confidence.

semantic_domains.cognition: advance anticipation of an event or pattern from prior information
semantic_domains.temporal: advance knowability of timing or sequence
